issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling, which may expose sensitive stack traces or error details in unstructured logs. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but lacks structured logging.
issues.suggestion: Use a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) with appropriate log levels and sanitized error messages to avoid leaking sensitive information.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to authentication endpoints (join, login, refresh). This allows brute-force attacks against credentials.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to /auth/member/login, /auth/member/join, and /auth/member/refresh endpoints at the infrastructure or middleware level.

issues.description: Password complexity validation is mentioned in the spec comment but not enforced at the controller level. AutoBE does not generate password strength checks beyond type validation.
issues.suggestion: Add server-side password strength validation (minimum length, character classes) in the join provider or via a custom validation pipe.

issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide structured logging, log levels, or correlation IDs for production monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Consider integrating a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) with log levels and request correlation IDs across all controllers.

issues.description: The timer discard endpoint (DELETE /timers/:timerId) specification states that time:manage permission holders can discard any employee's timer, but the implementation spec (step 4) contradicts this by only allowing the timer owner. This business logic inconsistency could lead to either unauthorized access or missing legitimate admin functionality.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider implementation consistently enforces the intended access control: either allow time:manage holders to discard any timer (as stated in the description) or restrict to owner-only (as stated in the spec steps). Align the documentation and implementation.

issues.description: The organizationId path parameter is accepted from the client but the authorization check relies on the member's session-resolved organization context. If the provider does not strictly validate that the authenticated member's organization matches the path organizationId, a member could potentially manipulate permissions in a different organization by supplying a foreign organizationId.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the provider explicitly validates that the authenticated member belongs to the organizationId specified in the path parameter before performing any role/permission operations. Cross-tenant isolation must be enforced at the service layer.

issues.description: The projectId path parameter is used to scope task operations, but if the provider does not validate that the projectId belongs to the authenticated member's organization, a member could access or modify tasks in projects belonging to other organizations by guessing project UUIDs.
issues.suggestion: Ensure all task providers validate that the project identified by projectId belongs to the same organization as the authenticated member before performing any operations.

issues.description: The endpoint resolves the caller's organization using findFirstOrThrow without specifying which organization to use when a member belongs to multiple organizations. This could return an arbitrary organization membership, potentially leaking data from an unintended organization context.
issues.suggestion: Require an explicit organizationId parameter in the request body or path, and filter by both member_id and organization_id to ensure deterministic organization resolution.

issues.description: The organization member creation uses props.member.id (the caller's member ID) instead of props.body.memberId when linking the new member record. This means the caller is always recorded as the creator, but more critically, the authorization check is missing — there is no verification that the caller has employee:manage or org:manage permission before creating a new organization member.
issues.suggestion: Add a permission check (employee:manage or org:manage) before creating the organization member. Also verify the data passed to the collector uses the correct memberId from the body, not the caller's ID.

issues.description: The session lookup does not filter out already-expired sessions before the explicit expiry check. A race condition exists: between the session lookup and the expiry update, another concurrent refresh could succeed with the same session, enabling token replay. The old session is invalidated by setting expired_at to now, but a concurrent request that already passed the expiry check could still proceed.
issues.suggestion: Use a database-level atomic update with a WHERE clause that checks expired_at > now() to invalidate the session, and only proceed if the update affected exactly one row. This prevents concurrent refresh token reuse.

issues.description: When resolving pending invitations, the code automatically assigns the 'Employee' built-in role to the new member without verifying that the invitation was legitimately sent to this email address by an authorized person. An attacker who knows a pending invitation exists for an email could register with that email and gain organization access.
issues.suggestion: This is partially mitigated by the invitation status check, but consider adding invitation token verification (a signed token sent to the email) to confirm the registrant actually received the invitation email before auto-accepting it.

issues.description: The JWT verification does not validate the 'type' claim or any other custom claims beyond signature and expiry. An access token and refresh token share the same secret and issuer, so a refresh token could potentially be used as an access token if the controller does not separately validate the token type.
issues.suggestion: Add explicit validation of the 'type' claim in jwtAuthorize to ensure only 'member' access tokens (not refresh tokens) are accepted for API authorization. Check that type === 'member' and that the token does not contain a 'tokenType: refresh' claim.

issues.description: The organization switch endpoint does not issue a new JWT token scoped to the target organization. If the JWT payload contains organization context, switching organizations without re-issuing a token means the old token remains valid for the previous organization context, potentially causing authorization bypass in downstream checks that rely on JWT claims for organization scoping.
issues.suggestion: If organization context is embedded in JWT claims, issue a new access token upon organization switch. If organization context is always resolved from the database (not JWT), document this clearly and ensure all authorization checks use DB-resolved context.

issues.description: Registration events and invitation auto-acceptance are not logged to the activity log, making it difficult to audit account creation and organization membership grants.
issues.suggestion: Add audit log entries for member registration and invitation acceptance events, including IP address and timestamp.

issues.description: No rate limiting on the registration endpoint. Automated account creation could be used to enumerate valid email addresses via the 409 conflict response.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure level (e.g., API gateway or NestJS ThrottlerModule) and consider returning a generic message that does not distinguish between 'email taken' and other errors.

issues.description: No password complexity validation is performed beyond type checking. Weak passwords are accepted.
issues.suggestion: Enforce minimum password length and complexity requirements (uppercase, lowercase, digit, special character) using a validation pipe or custom validator.

issues.description: Access and refresh tokens are both signed with the same JWT_SECRET_KEY and only differentiated by a 'tokenType' claim. If the secret is compromised, both token types are exposed. Additionally, there is no algorithm explicitly specified in jwt.sign(), defaulting to HS256 which is acceptable but should be explicit.
issues.suggestion: Use separate secrets for access and refresh tokens (e.g., JWT_ACCESS_SECRET and JWT_REFRESH_SECRET), and explicitly set the algorithm: { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '1h' }.

issues.description: Session expiry is hardcoded to 30 days with no mechanism to invalidate sessions server-side (e.g., on logout or password change). The session record is append-only and logout is handled only via expired_at, meaning a compromised session token remains valid until natural expiry.
issues.suggestion: Add a 'revoked_at' or 'is_active' field to sessions and check it during token validation. On logout or password change, mark the session as revoked.

issues.description: Failed login attempts (wrong password or unknown email) are not logged. This makes it impossible to detect brute-force attacks or credential stuffing in monitoring systems.
issues.suggestion: Log failed authentication attempts with timestamp and IP address (props.ip) to an audit log, without logging the attempted password.

issues.description: The activity log 'details' field is constructed via string concatenation: `start_date=${props.body.startDate};pay_period=${props.body.payPeriod}`. If these values contain semicolons or special characters, the log entry could be misleading or difficult to parse reliably.
issues.suggestion: Store structured data (e.g., JSON) in the details field instead of a delimited string to ensure integrity and parseability of audit logs.

issues.description: The authorization check verifies 'project:manage' permission OR 'project-lead' role, but does not verify that the project belongs to the same organization as the calling member. A member from a different organization who somehow knows the projectId could potentially manipulate tasks if the organization_id check on the project is not enforced upstream.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the project's organization_id matches the caller's organization_id before proceeding with authorization checks. Add: if (project.organization_id !== orgMember.organization_id) throw new HttpException('Forbidden', 403).

issues.description: The timesheet rejection endpoint does not validate the length of rejection_reason beyond checking it is non-empty. An excessively long rejection reason could cause database issues or be used for data bloat.
issues.suggestion: Add a maximum length validation on rejection_reason (e.g., max 1000 characters) using typia or manual validation.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/118 files] [4 chunks, 26→22 issues] The authentication controller follows good security practices: bcrypt password hashing is specified, JWT token pairs with short-lived access tokens are used, user enumeration is prevented on login failures, and session management is implemented. The timesheet and project controllers properly use MemberAuth decorators for authentication and enforce permission-based access control (time:approve, project:manage, etc.) with ownership validation. The main concerns are infrastructure-level: missing rate limiting on auth endpoints, unstructured error logging via console.log, and no explicit password complexity enforcement. No critical vulnerabilities such as injection, IDOR, or broken access control were identified in the controller layer.

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across all files: IEntity, IErpHrmDepartment, IErpHrmMember, IErpHrmOrganizationMember, IErpHrmProject, IErpHrmRole, IErpHrmRolePermission, IErpHrmTask, IErpHrmTimelog, IErpHrmTimesheet, IErpHrmTimer, IErpHrmTaskHistory, IErpHrmProjectMember, IErpHrmGuest, IErpHrmGuestSession, IErpHrmInvitation, IErpHrmOrganization, ArrayUtil, Prisma, jwt, typia, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe. These imports are not used in the functions.

issues.description: The query uses 'owner' relation but the Prisma model likely doesn't have a field named 'owner'. Should be referencing the organization_member relation.
issues.suggestion: Change 'owner' to the correct relation name, likely 'organization_member'.

issues.description: The query references 'organizationMember' relation but uses incorrect field name 'organizationMember.member_id'. Should check the actual relation structure.
issues.suggestion: Verify the Prisma schema to ensure correct relation field names.

issues.description: The query uses 'erp_hrm_project_id' field which follows the prefixed convention, but other parts of the code use 'project_id'. Need consistency.
issues.suggestion: Check Prisma schema for correct field names and use consistent naming.

issues.description: Function updates member's 'updated_at' but returns a guest session. This seems like a requirements mismatch - profile update should return member/profile data, not guest session.
issues.suggestion: Return appropriate member or profile data instead of guest session.

issues.description: Function updates the caller's own organization member record based on 'callerMember.id', but the endpoint name suggests it should update organization members in general. This may be a requirements mismatch.
issues.suggestion: Review requirements - should this endpoint update any organization member or only the caller's own record?

issues.description: Inconsistent parameter naming: some functions use camelCase (projectId) while others use snake_case (project_id). The DTO interface IErpHrmTimelog.IUpdate uses snake_case but function parameters should follow TypeScript conventions.
issues.suggestion: Standardize on camelCase for TypeScript/JavaScript interfaces and parameters.

issues.description: Importing from non-existent module '@prisma/sdk'. The correct import for Prisma types is '@prisma/client'.
issues.suggestion: Change import { Prisma } from '@prisma/sdk'; to import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';

issues.description: Multiple unused imports across all files (IEntity, IErpHrmDepartment, IErpHrmMember, IErpHrmProject, IErpHrmRole, IErpHrmRolePermission, IErpHrmTask, IErpHrmTimelog, ArrayUtil, jwt, typia, v4, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe). These imports are not used in the function bodies.
issues.suggestion: Remove all unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: The query for 'owner' relation on timesheet uses 'owner' field which likely doesn't exist. Based on the schema, the relationship should be through 'organization_member_id' to 'erp_hrm_organization_members'.
issues.suggestion: Change the select clause to join through the correct relation: 'organizationMember: { select: { organization_id: true } }' instead of 'owner'.

issues.description: The query uses a composite unique constraint 'organization_id_member_id' which may not exist. The correct constraint for finding an organization member by organization and member is likely a combination of 'organization_id' and 'member_id' fields.
issues.suggestion: Change the where clause to use separate fields: where: { organization_id: timerOwnerOrgMember.organization_id, member_id: props.member.id }

issues.description: The work_date calculation uses Date.UTC with getUTCMonth() which is correct, but the month parameter in Date.UTC is 0-indexed (January = 0). The code uses getUTCMonth() directly which is correct, but this subtlety could lead to errors if misunderstood.
issues.suggestion: Add a comment explaining the 0-indexed month to prevent future errors.

issues.description: The validation for assignee_id checks if the assignee is a project member, but it uses 'props.body.assignee_id' which is an OrganizationMember ID, not a ProjectMember ID. The check should verify the OrganizationMember is a ProjectMember.
issues.suggestion: The current check is correct as it looks for a ProjectMember with the given organization_member_id. No change needed, but the description clarifies the logic.

issues.description: The permission check uses 'callerMember.role.permissions' which assumes eager loading. The query doesn't include 'role.permissions' in the select, so this will cause a runtime error.
issues.suggestion: Update the select clause to include 'role: { select: { permissions: { select: { permission_code: true } } } }' or fetch permissions separately.

issues.description: All provider files have identical import blocks with many unused imports, suggesting copy-paste without cleanup.
issues.suggestion: Standardize imports and remove unused ones in each file individually.

issues.description: Importing from non-existent module '@ORGANIZATION/PROJECT-api/lib/structures/IEntity' and similar paths. These imports appear to be placeholders that won't resolve in a real project.
issues.suggestion: Replace with actual import paths or ensure the API library is properly configured. The imports should point to real TypeScript interfaces generated by the project.
